channel.title: Kurt Mueller
channel.description: A blog about F# and functional programming

item.title: Welcome
item.description:
``` {.f# .cell-code}
title: “Hello, F# World”
description: “My first blog post using F# and Jupyter notebooks”
author: “Kurt Mueller”
date: “2026-03-29”
categories:
- fsharp
- getting-started 

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error}

Error: input.fsx (2,1)-(2,6) parse error Invalid prefix operator input.fsx (2,6)-(2,7) parse error Unexpected symbol ‘:’ in interaction. Expected ‘;’, ‘;;’ or other token. input.fsx (2,6)-(2,7) interactive error parse error input.fsx (2,1)-(2,6) parse error Invalid prefix operator

input.fsx (2,6)-(2,7) parse error Unexpected symbol ‘:’ in interaction. Expected ‘;’, ‘;;’ or other token.

input.fsx (2,6)-(2,7) interactive error parse error

:::
:::



This is a sample blog post written as a Jupyter notebook using the F# kernel.
Let's start with a simple example.


::: {#cell-3 .cell quarto-private-1='{"key":"dotnet_interactive","value":{"language":"fsharp"}}' execution_count=2}
``` {.f# .cell-code}
let greeting = "Hello from F#!"
printfn "%s" greeting
Hello from F#!

F# is a functional-first language that runs on .NET. It’s great for data exploration, scripting, and building robust applications.

Here’s a quick example of pattern matching:

``` {.f# .cell-code} type Shape = | Circle of radius: float | Rectangle of width: float * height: float

let area shape = match shape with | Circle radius -> System.Math.PI * radius * radius | Rectangle (width, height) -> width * height

let shapes = [ Circle 5.0; Rectangle (3.0, 4.0) ]

shapes |> List.iter (fun s -> printfn “%A has area %.2f” s (area s))


::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

Circle 5.0 has area 78.54 Rectangle (3.0, 4.0) has area 12.00 ```

:::
